title: British Time Expressions You Need Every Day ⏰

vocab.definition: to start or continue doing something quickly and with energy
vocab.example: Let's crack on with the lesson.

vocab.definition: an unreasonably early or late hour of the day
vocab.example: I woke up at stupid o'clock this morning.
I haven't seen you in yonks!

vocab.definition: to spend time doing things that are not important without achieving much
vocab.example: Stop faffing around and get ready.
We go out for dinner now and then.

vocab.definition: approaching a certain age, time, or number
vocab.example: It is getting on for midnight.

lines.text: Hello everyone and welcome back to Sprout English.
Today we are looking at British time expressions that you will hear every day.
Let's crack on with the first phrase before we run out of time.
I had to wake up at stupid o'clock this morning to catch my train.
Honestly, I haven't been to this part of the city in absolute yonks.
If you stop faffing around, we might actually finish this work early.
We go down to the local pub now and then just to catch up.
Look at the clock, it is getting on for ten already, we should go.

vocab.definition: an expression of surprise or excitement
vocab.example: I felt whoa when I saw the rollercoaster's steep drop

vocab.definition: feeling a sense of giddiness or lightheadedness
vocab.example: After spinning around, I felt dizzy and had to sit down

vocab.definition: careful and precise in one's work or actions
vocab.example: The meticulous artist spent hours perfecting each brushstroke
The bike was stationary, parked outside the shop

vocab.definition: taking advantage of opportunities as they arise
vocab.example: The entrepreneur was opportunistic, seizing new business ideas
The fireworks display was phenomenal, lighting up the night sky

lines.text: Good morning.
Whoa, dizzy.
Dizzy.
I feel dizzy.
Good morning, you beautiful people, and welcome.
This is my first ever vlog.
And today I'm going to get my hair cut.
I'm gonna take you guys with me and teach you English using the world around me.
I've got my bag on.
I'm ready to go.
Let's go get a haircut.
Here we go.
Hello.
I'm putting on my helmet to protect my head.
And with one hand, I'm fattening the strap.
Good to go.
Key.
I'm putting the key in the ignition and turning it.
I am setting off on my journey.
We have arrived.
Viking barber shop.
A barber shop is a hairdresser for men.
And a hairdresser for women would be a salon.
This bike is parked.
These bikes are parked.
They are stationary.
They are still.
All right, let's go.
for my free coffee.
So, I'm here getting my hair cut.
And we would say to get a hair cut, not to cut your hair.
If you say, "I'm going to cut my hair." It means that you're going to cut it yourself.
When you go to a barber shop, when you go to a hairdressers, you say, "I'm going to get a haircut." or I'm going to have a haircut soon in the future.
I will have a haircut.
So, right now I am sitting in the barber chair.
In front of me there is the sink and we have all the different tools, the equipment.
This is wax, hair wax.
This is a brush for your hair.
These are clippers.
Clippers.
Clippers.
These little things are called figurines.
You got three Spider-Man figurines.
Iron Man figurine and Goku there on the wall.
We have stickers.
The stickers are stuck to the wall.
Tan the man.
Yes, bro.
Okay.
About to get my long hair cut.
Now is blow drying my hair with the hair dryer.
All right, leaving the barber shop and walking out into the busy, hot Vietnam streets.
I am in the city of Daang.
I've just spotted a fruit market over there.
So, I'm going to go grab some fruits.
I'm just waiting for a safe moment to cross the road.
In Vietnam, when crossing a road, you have to be opportunistic.
What does that mean?
It means to take an opportunity when it arrives.
Just like now, I'm going to be opportunistic.
It could be borderline dangerous.
You never know what's coming in Vietnam, but we made it.
I'm across the road.
So, look here.
We have a little fruit market.
We got some avocados looking very green and beautiful.
The oranges or these are clementine or tangerines.
Here we have guava in the packets.
It's in a plastic packaging.
These are, I believe, bell fruit.
Bellfruit coming this way.
I'm not sure what these little things are.
No idea.
Looks like a small apple.
Grapes here.
These are bunches of grapes.
Next to the grapes, we have the dragon fruits.
These are white dragon fruits.
Inside the flesh is white.
Over here, inside the flesh of these bad boys is red.
The red dragon fruits and the white dragon fruits.
On the outside, the skin looks kind of the same, but I've lived in Vietnam long enough to know my red dragon fruit from my white dragon fruit.
Everything looks beautiful.
It all looks very colorful.
Very colorful.
You could say a rainbow of colors.
Anyway, I'm going to buy some now.
I want to buy some avocados.
I'm testing these.
They're very firm, very hard.
They're not yet ripe.
When a fruit is ripe, it means it's soft.
It's fresh, ready to eat.
Feeling good.
Let's move.
Look how fresh my haircut is.
The attention to detail that my barber Tuan pays is phenomenal.
He's meticulous.
Meticulous.
What does that mean?
He takes care when he does something.
Every single detail is important to him.
He is meticulous.
He's trimmed my eyebrows.
Before I had a mono brow.
Time to hop back on my motorbike.
We're going to go to a new location.
So, we're going on a little walk under the bridge here.
I'm going to walk through the bridge.
So all this area is shaded.
It's protected from the sun.
And I don't know if you can hear that, but when I speak like this ho, the sound bounces back off the walls.
Do you know what that's called?
That is called an echo.
A co a.
Let's take a walk through the gate and up the stairs.
Right now I am walking up the stairs.
I was just talking past continuous.
I was talking to you about the echoes and now I'm walking.
Present continuous.
I am walking up the stairs.
On either side of me, on either side, there is loud traffic.
Look at that.
There's a cool yellow van.
That's a yellow van.
And there was a man smoking a cigarette and he and he was very curious about what I was doing.
So what the what the hell is that white man doing in 40° heat?
Well, good question.
I have no idea.

And guys, what would be really useful for you guys when watching these videos, when you hear new vocabulary, when you hear me speak something that you have not heard before, maybe it will be a skyscraper, then it would be great if you could put that in your own sentence or if you could shadow me.
So basically when you hear me speak a new word that you have not heard before, shadow me.
Practice speaking that word.
Then not only are you learning the new word in context, but you're also producing it in context.

And this is basically going to boost the efficiency of these videos if you can do your part and put a little bit of effort in in producing the language as well as receiving it as well as listening to it and watching these videos.
I've showed you guys the beautiful Daang River.
This is called the Han River.
There is the Dragon Bridge.
I'm very lucky to live in Daang, Vietnam.
If you're watching this and you're from Daang, then let me know in the comments.

I'm very curious.
I would love to know.
